schema.label: Ongoing Flexibility — Add / Sell / Borrow Later
Typical Combination with Other Trusts & Strategies

trusts.name: Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT)
trusts.primaryPurpose: Use the lifetime exemption now without the household losing access to the money

attrs.primaryPurpose: Use the lifetime exemption now without the household losing access to the money
attrs.name: Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT)
attrs.howItWorks: One spouse gifts assets to an irrevocable trust naming the other spouse (and descendants) as beneficiaries. The beneficiary spouse can receive distributions, so the household keeps indirect access while the assets and all future growth sit outside both estates. Example: wife gifts $10M of index funds; at 7% that is ~$38.7M in 20 years — the ~$28.7M of growth escapes the 40% estate tax (~$11.5M saved) on top of the exemption locked in.
attrs.typicalClient: Married couple, roughly $10M-$60M net worth, wants to use the exemption but is nervous about irrevocably giving money away
attrs.usesExemption: Yes — this is usually the primary exemption-use vehicle
attrs.inEstate: No, if drafted and administered correctly (avoid reciprocal mirror-image trusts when both spouses create SLATs)
attrs.basisStepUp: No — carryover basis; use the swap power to manage which assets die inside
attrs.incomeTaxPayer: Grantor — it is a grantor trust, so the grantor pays tax on trust income; each tax payment is itself an extra tax-free gift to the trust
attrs.failureModes: Reciprocal trusts uncrossed (mirror-image SLATs); divorce with no floating-spouse clause; electing gift-splitting despite the spouse being a beneficiary; distributions drifting back to the grantor's own benefit
attrs.mortalityRisk: Low — the grantor's death is fine; the real risk is the beneficiary spouse dying first or divorce, either of which cuts off indirect access
attrs.bestAssets: High-growth marketable securities, LP/LLC interests, cash the trust uses to buy life insurance
attrs.gstStatus: Can be fully GST exempt — allocate exemption on a timely-filed Form 709 at the gift; no Estate Tax Inclusion Period (ETIP) issue
attrs.qsbsFit: No — a SLAT is a grantor trust: grantor and trust share ONE Section 1202 cap. Holding QSBS inside is fine; multiplying the exclusion is not — the SLANT in the next column is the engineered fix
attrs.hurdleRate: None at the gift (fair market value). The Applicable Federal Rate (AFR) applies only to later loans or sales into the trust
attrs.flexibility: Open — add by further gift; sell assets to it income-tax-free (grantor trust); the trust can loan the grantor money at the Applicable Federal Rate (AFR) if the instrument allows. The escape valves exist — paper them like real transactions
attrs.swapPower: Standard — THE basis-management tool: swap cash or high-basis assets in for the trust's low-basis winners before death
attrs.annualCost: $1K-$5K with a family trustee; a corporate trustee adds roughly $5K-$25K+ depending on assets
attrs.setupCost: $15K-$40K for a reciprocal-proofed pair; single SLAT toward the low end
attrs.ageToImplement: 35-60 — young enough that decades of compounding happen outside the estate; both spouses healthy
attrs.duration: Beneficiary spouse's lifetime, then continues for descendants — usually drafted with dynasty provisions
attrs.beneficiaries: Grantor's spouse; children and later descendants
attrs.fundingMethod: Completed gift reported on Form 709. Fund with the growth assets; keep the basis-heavy ones outside for the step-up
attrs.combinations: Dynasty provisions built in; the trust often buys life insurance (SLAT/ILIT hybrid); later installment sales into it as an Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT); pairs with annual-exclusion gifting programs; runs beside a SLANT in founder households (one of each)

trusts.name: Spousal Lifetime Access Non-Grantor Trust (SLANT)
trusts.primaryPurpose: Use the exemption with spousal access — while the trust stays its OWN taxpayer (for QSBS stacking and state-income-tax planning)

attrs.primaryPurpose: Use the exemption with spousal access — while the trust stays its OWN taxpayer (for QSBS stacking and state-income-tax planning)
attrs.name: Spousal Lifetime Access Non-Grantor Trust (SLANT)
attrs.howItWorks: The SLAT chassis drafted to FAIL grantor-trust status: distributions to the spouse require an adverse party's consent, so Section 677 never attaches. The trust becomes a separate taxpayer — it stacks its own Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS) exclusion, and, sitused out of state with no California fiduciaries and only discretionary California beneficiaries, its undistributed non-California-source income can escape California tax (accumulated income faces throwback when later distributed). The trade: the grantor's tax-free burn is gone and retained income hits compressed trust brackets. Example: founder's spouse gifts $8M of QSBS to a SLANT — a second $10M-$15M Section 1202 exclusion at exit, worth roughly $2.4M-$3.6M of federal tax, while the household keeps consent-gated access.
attrs.typicalClient: Founder households wanting SLAT-style access PLUS a second QSBS exclusion; high-tax-state residents with large undistributed portfolio income
attrs.inEstate: No, if drafted and administered correctly — same reciprocal-trust cautions as SLATs
attrs.basisStepUp: No — carryover basis, and WITHOUT a swap power there is no clean way to pull low-basis assets back before death. Plan basis at funding, not later
attrs.incomeTaxPayer: The trust itself — non-grantor status is the point, achieved by requiring an ADVERSE party's consent (typically an adult child / remainder beneficiary) for any distribution to the spouse. Compressed trust brackets apply: top 37% federal rate at roughly $16K of retained income, plus the 3.8% net investment income tax
attrs.failureModes: Accidental grantor-trust taint — a swap power, a non-adverse consent party, spousal powers, or loans without adequate security each flip it; a California trustee quietly re-attaches California tax; heavy yield parked at 37%+ trust brackets; the adverse party refusing consent when the spouse actually needs money
attrs.mortalityRisk: Low — same profile as the SLAT: the exposure is the beneficiary spouse's death or divorce, not the grantor's
attrs.bestAssets: Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS) above all; otherwise high-growth, low-current-income assets (compressed trust brackets punish yield)
attrs.gstStatus: Can be fully GST exempt — allocate on a timely Form 709 at the gift; no ETIP issue
attrs.qsbsFit: YES — this is the SLAT fix: a separate taxpayer with its own Section 1202 cap ($10M or $15M by issue date) while preserving spousal access. California still taxes the gain (no conformity)
attrs.hurdleRate: None at the gift (fair market value) — and note there is no AFR machinery to lean on: sales into it are TAXABLE, since there is no grantor-trust invisibility
attrs.flexibility: Guarded — additions by gift are fine, but SALES to it recognize gain (no grantor invisibility) and loans to the grantor risk grantor-trust taint; every distribution to the spouse needs the adverse party's signature
attrs.assetProtection: Strong — same third-party spendthrift protection as a SLAT
attrs.swapPower: NO — a swap power would flip it to grantor status and destroy both the stacking and the state-tax position
attrs.annualCost: $4K-$10K — its own Form 1041, state filings, ongoing residency monitoring
attrs.setupCost: $20K-$45K — SLAT drafting plus the non-grantor engineering
attrs.ageToImplement: 30s-50s — gift while the valuation is low and the exit is still ahead
attrs.duration: Spouse's lifetime, then descendants — dynasty provisions common
attrs.beneficiaries: Grantor's spouse (distributions only with adverse-party consent) plus descendants
attrs.fundingMethod: Completed gift reported on Form 709 — QSBS or high-growth assets; fund early while valuations are low
attrs.complexity: High — non-grantor status must survive the grantor-trust rules (Sections 672-677); adverse-party mechanics and fiduciary/beneficiary residency need permanent discipline
attrs.popularity: Medium — rising fast, especially among founders
attrs.combinations: Runs beside a classic SLAT in founder households (one of each: burn and flexibility in the SLAT, stacking and the state play in the SLANT); dynasty provisions; coordinates with the founder's own Section 1202 exclusion and charitable share gifts

trusts.name: Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT)
trusts.primaryPurpose: Keep life insurance proceeds out of the taxable estate and create liquidity to pay estate tax

attrs.primaryPurpose: Keep life insurance proceeds out of the taxable estate and create liquidity to pay estate tax
attrs.name: Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT)
attrs.howItWorks: The trust owns a policy on the grantor's life. The grantor makes annual exclusion gifts ($19,000 per beneficiary in 2026) to cover premiums; beneficiaries receive short-term Crummey withdrawal rights so the gifts qualify for the exclusion. At death, the benefit is paid income-tax-free and estate-tax-free, and the trust can lend to or buy assets from the estate to fund the tax bill. Example: $40K/yr premium supports a $5M policy — $2M of estate tax avoided at 40%, and no fire sale of the business to pay the IRS.
attrs.typicalClient: Anyone with a taxable estate and an insurance need — especially illiquid estates built on an operating business or real estate
attrs.usesExemption: Minimal — mostly annual exclusion gifts; large single-premium designs may consume some exemption
attrs.inEstate: No, if the trust owns the policy from inception and the grantor keeps no incidents of ownership; an existing policy transferred in stays in the estate for 3 years
attrs.basisStepUp: Not relevant — the death benefit is income-tax-free regardless
attrs.incomeTaxPayer: Grantor (typically a grantor trust; the trust mostly holds a policy, so there is little current income to tax anyway)
attrs.failureModes: Missed or undocumented Crummey notices; grantor paying premiums directly; retained incidents of ownership; the 3-year rule on transferred policies; nobody monitoring the policy until it lapses
attrs.mortalityRisk: Low — early death is actually the trust's best financial outcome; the one mortality trap is dying within 3 years of transferring an existing policy into the trust
attrs.gstStatus: Usually GST exempt via annual allocation to premium gifts — a small exemption shields a large death benefit (high leverage). Watch the automatic-allocation rules on Form 709
attrs.qsbsFit: No — not the vehicle; it holds insurance, not stock
attrs.hurdleRate: None — insurance pricing drives the economics, not IRS rate tables
attrs.flexibility: Add premium gifts annually; at death the trust can buy assets from or lend to the estate — that is the liquidity play. Avoid casual borrowing against the policy (incidents-of-ownership optics)
attrs.swapPower: Common — often the very power that makes it a grantor trust; rarely exercised in practice
attrs.annualCost: $1K-$3K plus Crummey administration discipline; a corporate trustee adds $2.5K-$7.5K
attrs.ageToImplement: 40-65 — insurability drives timing; premiums price off health, not the calendar
attrs.duration: Insured's lifetime plus payout; often drafted as a dynasty trust
attrs.fundingMethod: The trust itself applies for a new policy; premiums funded by annual-exclusion (Crummey) gifts. Transferring an existing policy works but starts the 3-year inclusion clock
attrs.complexity: Low-Medium (Crummey withdrawal notices are the main administrative discipline)
attrs.combinations: Pairs with everything as the liquidity backstop; classic wealth-replacement partner to a Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT); frequently merged into a Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT)

trusts.name: Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT)
trusts.primaryPurpose: Transfer the upside of a volatile asset above the IRS hurdle rate using little or no gift tax

attrs.primaryPurpose: Transfer the upside of a volatile asset above the IRS hurdle rate using little or no gift tax
attrs.name: Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT)
attrs.howItWorks: The grantor funds the trust and retains an annuity that returns the full principal plus interest at the Section 7520 rate. Anything the assets earn above that hurdle passes to heirs free of gift tax; a zeroed-out GRAT uses essentially no exemption. Example: $10M of pre-IPO stock in a 2-year GRAT; if the stock doubles, roughly $8M+ lands with heirs at ~$0 gift-tax cost. If it underperforms, the grantor simply gets everything back — heads you win, tails you break even (minus fees).
attrs.typicalClient: Founder or executive holding concentrated, volatile, appreciating stock who wants to keep the exemption dry for other moves
attrs.usesExemption: No — the zeroed-out design uses essentially none
attrs.inEstate: Yes during the term — if the grantor dies mid-term, most of the value is pulled back into the estate
attrs.basisStepUp: No for the remainder passing to heirs
attrs.failureModes: Late or mis-valued in-kind annuity payments; death during the term; failing to swap into safety after a big run-up; terms longer than the mortality math supports
attrs.mortalityRisk: High — dying during the term defeats the trust; that is exactly why terms are kept short and rolling
attrs.bestAssets: Volatile, high-upside positions: pre-IPO shares, concentrated public stock, assets about to reprice
attrs.gstStatus: Poor — the Estate Tax Inclusion Period (ETIP) blocks GST allocation until the term ends, and by then the assets have appreciated, making exemption expensive. Not a grandchild vehicle — see the ETIP tab
attrs.qsbsFit: No — grantor trust, no separate Section 1202 cap; and the remainder timing rarely lines up with an exit
attrs.hurdleRate: IRS assumed interest rate used to value trust (IRS Section 7520 Rate) — 5.2% in July 2026. The annuity must outperform it; every basis point of rate is headwind
attrs.flexibility: Closed — no additions ever (regulation); no borrowing role; the annuity already returns money on a fixed schedule
attrs.assetProtection: Weak during the term (the grantor's retained annuity is reachable by creditors)
attrs.swapPower: Yes — and underused: after a big run-up, swap the winner for bonds to lock the transfer in mid-term (immunizing the GRAT)
attrs.annualCost: $2K-$6K per year during the term (annuity valuations and payment mechanics)
attrs.setupCost: $10K-$25K for the first; $2.5K-$7.5K per re-roll thereafter
attrs.ageToImplement: Any age with the right asset — short 2-3 year terms make even 70+ workable
attrs.duration: Short — 2-3 year terms are typical, usually rolling (each annuity payment funds a new GRAT)
attrs.beneficiaries: Grantor receives the annuity; children (or trusts for them) take the remainder
attrs.fundingMethod: Single contribution at signing; additional contributions are prohibited by regulation — new money means a new GRAT
attrs.complexity: Medium — the regulations are precise and annuity payments must be made exactly on schedule
attrs.combinations: Rolling GRAT ladders; the remainder often pours into a dynasty trust or continues inside a grantor trust

trusts.name: Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT) — usually via installment sale
trusts.primaryPurpose: Freeze the estate — swap a growing asset for a note that grows at only the Applicable Federal Rate (AFR)

attrs.primaryPurpose: Freeze the estate — swap a growing asset for a note that grows at only the Applicable Federal Rate (AFR)
attrs.name: Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT) — usually via installment sale
attrs.howItWorks: The grantor gifts seed capital (uses exemption), then sells assets to the trust for an installment note at the AFR. Because it is a grantor trust, the sale triggers no capital gain. Growth above the note rate accumulates outside the estate, and the estate shrinks further every year the grantor pays the trust's income taxes. Example: seed $2M, sell $20M of discounted family LLC interests on a 4.98% note; at 9% asset growth, well over $20M of value shifts out over 15 years beyond the note payments.
attrs.typicalClient: $30M+ net worth with cash-flowing business, real estate, or fund interests; valuation discounts available on the asset sold
attrs.usesExemption: Yes — but only the ~10% seed gift; the sale itself uses none
attrs.inEstate: Trust assets: no. The unpaid note balance: yes — this is a freeze, not a removal
attrs.basisStepUp: No — and dying with the note outstanding raises unsettled income-tax questions; plan to retire the note during life
attrs.incomeTaxPayer: Grantor — that is the intentional defect, and it is a feature: paying the trust's income tax is an additional tax-free gift every year. For California grantors that includes 13.3% state tax — a bigger burn, and a bigger transfer
attrs.failureModes: Seed under ~10%; no independent appraisal; note payments skipped or informal (invites a Section 2036 retained-interest attack); dying with the note outstanding
attrs.mortalityRisk: Medium — death with the note outstanding is messy: possible gain recognition and the note sits in the estate
attrs.bestAssets: Cash-flowing, discountable assets: family LLC/LP interests, operating businesses, real estate, GP/carried interests (mind the Section 2701 vertical-slice rule)
attrs.gstStatus: Excellent — allocate GST exemption to the small seed gift at funding; the leveraged sale multiplies what that exemption shields. No ETIP issue
attrs.qsbsFit: No for stacking — grantor trust, no separate cap. But a fine QSBS holding vehicle: gifts (and sales, which are income-tax invisible to a grantor trust) preserve QSBS status and holding period
attrs.hurdleRate: Applicable Federal Rate (AFR) on the note — long-term 4.98% in July 2026. The note rate sitting below the Section 7520 rate is one reason sales beat GRATs at the margin
attrs.flexibility: Built for transactions — the sale IS the strategy; later sales and AFR loans are routine, and refinancing the note when rates drop is a real lever
attrs.swapPower: Standard — and the main defense against the lost basis step-up: swap high-basis assets in before death
attrs.annualCost: $3K-$10K (note servicing, grantor tax letter, periodic appraisal refreshes)
attrs.setupCost: $30K-$75K+ all-in, of which $10K-$25K is the appraisal
attrs.ageToImplement: 45-70 — needs mature cash-flowing assets, and the note should be retired during life
attrs.duration: Long — typically drafted as a perpetual dynasty trust
attrs.beneficiaries: Descendants; the spouse may be included, creating a SLAT/IDGT hybrid
attrs.fundingMethod: Two-step: seed gift (~10% of the sale price) reported on Form 709, then an installment sale for an interest-only or amortizing note at the Applicable Federal Rate (AFR)
attrs.complexity: High — seed capital (~10% of purchase price), note terms, appraisal support, and retained-control traps all need care
attrs.combinations: Almost always sits on a dynasty-trust chassis; often follows a Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT); pairs with family LLC / Family Limited Partnership (FLP) valuation discounts

trusts.name: Dynasty Trust
trusts.primaryPurpose: Let wealth compound for multiple generations without a 40% transfer-tax haircut at each one

attrs.primaryPurpose: Let wealth compound for multiple generations without a 40% transfer-tax haircut at each one
attrs.name: Dynasty Trust
attrs.howItWorks: Gift assets and allocate both gift and GST exemption; the trust then sits outside the transfer-tax system for every generation. Compare: $15M taxed at 40% at each of three generational hand-offs leaves ~$3.2M; the same $15M GST-exempt compounds untouched — at 6% for 75 years it is roughly $1.2B, none of it estate-taxed. Flexible beneficiary definitions also absorb uncertainty about future children or grandchildren.
attrs.typicalClient: $30M+ families with genuine multi-generational intent and the appetite for real governance
attrs.usesExemption: Yes — gift exemption and Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GST) exemption, usually in full
attrs.basisStepUp: No — manage around it with grantor-trust swap powers and by parking the highest-growth assets here
attrs.incomeTaxPayer: Grantor while alive (if drafted as a grantor trust); afterwards the trust or its beneficiaries. Drafted NON-grantor, the trust is its own taxpayer — which is what QSBS stacking requires
attrs.failureModes: California trustee or situs by accident; no decanting or trust-protector flexibility; GST allocation errors on Form 709; a governance vacuum by generation three
attrs.bestAssets: The highest-expected-growth assets the family owns: founders stock, carried interest, early-stage holdings
attrs.gstStatus: The entire point — allocate gift AND GST exemption at funding on Form 709; exempt at every later generation. No ETIP issue
attrs.qsbsFit: YES — the classic stacking chassis when drafted NON-grantor: each trust is a separate taxpayer with its own Section 1202 cap ($10M for stock issued on or before July 4, 2025; $15M after). Gift shares early at low value. California taxes the gain regardless — no state conformity
attrs.hurdleRate: None at funding; the Applicable Federal Rate (AFR) governs the family-bank loans it makes later
attrs.flexibility: Open while the grantor lives — add gifts (allocate GST each time), sell assets to it, and lend from it at the Applicable Federal Rate (AFR) as the family bank
attrs.assetProtection: Excellent — often the strongest of the group (spendthrift plus friendly state law)
attrs.swapPower: Standard while the grantor lives — but NOT in a non-grantor version built for QSBS stacking (the swap power is a grantor-trust trigger)
attrs.annualCost: $5K-$25K+ (situs trustee fees; directed-trustee structures run cheaper)
attrs.ageToImplement: 35-65 — the earliest possible funding maximizes the compounding runway
attrs.duration: Perpetual or near-perpetual — requires a friendly situs (South Dakota, Nevada, Delaware, Alaska). California's rule against perpetuities and fiduciary income tax make it a poor situs — house it out of state
attrs.beneficiaries: Children, grandchildren, and all later descendants
attrs.fundingMethod: Completed gift with gift and GST exemption allocated on a timely Form 709; older trusts sometimes decant in
attrs.complexity: High — situs selection, corporate or directed trustee structure, decanting powers, powers of appointment
attrs.popularity: Medium (as a stand-alone; very common as the chassis under SLATs and IDGTs)
attrs.combinations: Usually IS the chassis — Spousal Lifetime Access Trusts (SLATs), Irrevocable Life Insurance Trusts (ILITs), and Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT) sale trusts are commonly drafted as dynasty trusts

trusts.name: Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT)
trusts.primaryPurpose: Exit a concentrated low-basis position without immediate capital gains tax, keep lifetime income, leave the remainder to charity

attrs.primaryPurpose: Exit a concentrated low-basis position without immediate capital gains tax, keep lifetime income, leave the remainder to charity
attrs.name: Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT)
attrs.howItWorks: Contribute appreciated assets; the tax-exempt trust sells and diversifies with no immediate gain; the grantor receives (say) a 5-6% unitrust payout for life; charity keeps what is left, and the grantor takes an upfront deduction for the remainder's present value. Example: $5M of zero-basis stock — an outright sale loses ~$1.65M to federal plus California tax; inside a Charitable Remainder Unitrust (CRUT) the full $5M compounds and pays ~$250K+/yr for life.
attrs.typicalClient: Charitably inclined client with concentrated appreciated stock or real estate who wants income and diversification
attrs.usesExemption: No — this runs on the charitable deduction, not the gift exemption
attrs.inEstate: Removed — the income interest ends at death and the remainder passes to charity
attrs.basisStepUp: Not applicable — the trust is tax-exempt when it sells
attrs.incomeTaxPayer: The trust itself is tax-exempt; the income beneficiary pays tax on distributions as received (worst-in, first-out four-tier system)
attrs.failureModes: A pre-arranged binding sale before funding (assignment of income); flunking the 10% remainder test; contributing S-corp stock or mortgaged property; unrelated business taxable income (UBTI)
attrs.mortalityRisk: Low-Medium — early death means charity wins and family gets less; that is exactly why it pairs with life insurance
attrs.bestAssets: Low-basis public stock, appreciated real estate (avoid S-corporation stock and debt-encumbered property)
attrs.gstStatus: Mostly not applicable — the remainder goes to charity, not to grandchildren
attrs.qsbsFit: Not a stacking vehicle — the exclusion does not multiply through the four-tier distribution system. A CRT defers gain; Section 1202 excludes it. Different tools
attrs.hurdleRate: IRS assumed interest rate used to value trust (IRS Section 7520 Rate) — a high rate means a bigger upfront deduction and an easier 10% remainder test. Today's 5.2% is a tailwind
attrs.flexibility: Sealed — self-dealing rules bar sales and loans between the grantor and the trust. A CRUT can accept later additions; a CRAT cannot
attrs.swapPower: No — not a grantor trust, and a swap would be self-dealing
attrs.ageToImplement: 55-75 — the 10% remainder test passes more easily at older ages, and the income stream maps onto retirement
attrs.duration: One or two lives, or a fixed term of up to 20 years
attrs.beneficiaries: Grantor (and/or spouse) for the income interest; charity for the remainder
attrs.fundingMethod: Irrevocable contribution of the appreciated asset BEFORE any binding sale agreement exists
attrs.complexity: Medium — must pass the 10% minimum remainder test; payout between 5% and 50%; unitrust (CRUT) versus annuity (CRAT) choice
attrs.combinations: The classic pairing: an Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT) as a wealth-replacement trust so the heirs are made whole; the remainder can be a Donor-Advised Fund (DAF) the family advises

trusts.name: Qualified Personal Residence Trust (QPRT)
trusts.primaryPurpose: Move an expensive home to the children at a steep gift-tax discount while continuing to live in it

attrs.primaryPurpose: Move an expensive home to the children at a steep gift-tax discount while continuing to live in it
attrs.name: Qualified Personal Residence Trust (QPRT)
attrs.howItWorks: The grantor deeds the residence to the trust and keeps the right to live in it for a fixed term. The taxable gift is only the actuarial value of the remainder — heavily discounted at the current 5.2% rate. Survive the term and the house plus all appreciation is out of the estate; the grantor then pays fair-market rent, draining even more from the estate tax-free. Example: $6M home, 15-year term, grantor age 60 — taxable gift on the order of $2.5M; at 4% appreciation the house is ~$10.8M at term end, all outside the estate. California note: Prop 19 means the transfer at term end usually triggers property-tax reassessment unless a child occupies it as a primary residence within the exclusion limits — model both taxes.
attrs.typicalClient: Healthy client aged 55-70 with an expensive primary or vacation home and plenty of other liquidity
attrs.inEstate: Only if the grantor dies during the term (then the full value comes back into the estate)
attrs.basisStepUp: No — heirs take carryover basis, which stings on a long-held low-basis home; always weigh the lost step-up against the estate-tax saving
attrs.failureModes: A term the actuarial tables don't favor; no market-rate lease ready for post-term occupancy (a Section 2036 pull-back risk); ignoring the lost basis step-up and California Prop 19 reassessment in the math
attrs.mortalityRisk: High — dying during the term undoes the plan; pick a term the actuarial tables comfortably favor
attrs.bestAssets: Primary residence or one vacation home (each person may do one of each)
attrs.gstStatus: Poor — same Estate Tax Inclusion Period (ETIP) block as a GRAT: allocation only possible at term end, at the appreciated value. Not a grandchild vehicle — see the ETIP tab
attrs.hurdleRate: IRS assumed interest rate used to value trust (IRS Section 7520 Rate) — a high rate means a bigger retained-use discount and a smaller taxable gift. Today's 5.2% is a tailwind
attrs.flexibility: Closed — the trust may hold only the residence and limited cash, and the regulations bar the grantor from buying the house back
attrs.swapPower: No — the trust cannot hold substitute assets
attrs.annualCost: $1K-$3K during the term; lease administration afterward
attrs.ageToImplement: 55-70 sweet spot — old enough for a meaningful discount, young enough to comfortably outlive the term
attrs.duration: Retained term of 10-15 years is typical, then the residence passes (often into a continuing trust)
attrs.fundingMethod: Deed the residence into the trust; the retained occupancy term is set in the instrument; obtain a qualified appraisal
attrs.popularity: Medium — and rising when the Section 7520 rate is high, as now
attrs.combinations: Post-term rent paid into a grantor trust; typically runs alongside a Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT) / Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT) program

trusts.name: Charitable Lead Trust (CLT) — usually a zeroed-out Charitable Lead Annuity Trust (CLAT)
trusts.primaryPurpose: Fund charity now with an income stream and pass the remainder to heirs at a discounted (or zero) gift-tax value

attrs.primaryPurpose: Fund charity now with an income stream and pass the remainder to heirs at a discounted (or zero) gift-tax value
attrs.name: Charitable Lead Trust (CLT) — usually a zeroed-out Charitable Lead Annuity Trust (CLAT)
attrs.howItWorks: The mirror image of a Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT): charity receives an annuity for a term of years, and the heirs take whatever remains. The taxable gift is only the present value of the remainder; a zeroed-out CLAT sets the annuity so that value is ~$0, and any performance above the Section 7520 hurdle passes to heirs tax-free. Example: a $10M zeroed 20-year CLAT at 5.2% pays charity ~$820K/yr; if the assets earn 8.5%, roughly $8M-$9M still reaches the heirs at zero gift-tax cost.
attrs.typicalClient: Strongly charitable family that also wants heirs to receive a remainder; the grantor version suits a client with one unusually high-income year needing a large deduction
attrs.incomeTaxPayer: Grantor version: the grantor pays the trust's income tax but receives a large upfront charitable deduction. Non-grantor version: the trust pays its own tax and deducts its charitable payments
attrs.failureModes: Grantor dies mid-term (deduction recapture); annuity mis-sized for the zero-out; illiquid assets that cannot make the annuity payments; GST exemption allocated to a CLAT (see the GST and ETIP tabs)
attrs.mortalityRisk: Low-Medium — a grantor-CLAT death during the term triggers recapture of part of the upfront deduction
attrs.bestAssets: Diversified portfolios or assets expected to beat the Section 7520 hurdle; enough income or liquidity to cover the annuity
attrs.gstStatus: Awkward — a CLAT's GST allocation is effectively determined only at the end of the charitable term under the special Section 2642(e) rule, killing the leverage; a unitrust version (CLUT) can allocate at funding, at the cost of transfer efficiency
attrs.qsbsFit: A non-grantor CLT is technically its own taxpayer, but charity-first cash flows make it a poor Section 1202 stacking fit
attrs.hurdleRate: IRS assumed interest rate used to value trust (IRS Section 7520 Rate) — this is the hurdle the assets must beat, so LOW rates are the CLAT's friend. Today's 5.2% is a headwind
attrs.flexibility: Effectively closed — self-dealing rules apply to charitable lead trusts too; fund it right the first time
attrs.swapPower: Non-grantor version: no. Grantor CLAT: technically possible, rarely wise
attrs.ageToImplement: 50-70 — or any age in a one-time high-income year (grantor CLAT)
attrs.beneficiaries: Charity — or the family's Donor-Advised Fund (DAF) or private foundation — for the lead term; children for the remainder
attrs.fundingMethod: Single funding at term start with income-producing or readily diversifiable assets sized to cover the annuity
attrs.combinations: Lead interest often paid to the family's Donor-Advised Fund (DAF) or private foundation; usually sequenced after the exemption-using strategies
